---
title: "Alignment of Sequences"
author: "Ismael Maximiliano De Los Santos Huesca"
date: today

format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    geometry: margin=2cm
    fontsize: 10pt
    number-sections: true
    toc: false
    fig-cap-location: bottom

execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
  freeze: auto

project:
  type: default
  execute-dir: project

code-block-font-size: \footnotesize

engine: jupyter
jupyter:
  kernelspec:
    name: bio_informatics
    display_name: Python (bio_informatics)
    language: python

header-includes:
  - \usepackage{graphicx}
  - \usepackage{amsmath}
  - \usepackage{booktabs}
  - \usepackage{float}
  - \usepackage{caption}
  - \captionsetup{font=small}
---
# Alignment 
When you have the data clean and pre-proccesed we can asces the next step in the data processing, formaly we are talking about the alignment of the sequences to the reference genome, this references can be obtain by diferent data bases (like [Genecode](https://www.gencodegenes.org)) and it can have annotatations of the genes, this is highly depended in the study coverage of the genome, for our fortune we are working with one of the animal models used to study human. The *mus musculus* genome index is the [m36](https://www.gencodegenes.org/mouse/release_M36.html) realese available in genecode and indexed with the diferents programs that we are utilizing (hisat2 and star). 

## Comparison betwen aligners 
Whenn you are doing a Diferential expression analysis starting with the raw sequence data a key step in the data processing is the alignment to the reference genome, this is curcial because is the step where the information stored in the sequence data takes a biological sense in the way that we can identified what genomic possition the read is refering to (in so many cases genes) and this identification is the milestone in the conslusion we can made in the following analysis. 

For the above reasons it is important to compare diferent alignment tools not only for avoid the information lost also to make sure this information is sustented, it is known that there is not a perfect aligner and all alignments can not cover the 100% of the expression data, so the solution is to find the aligner that fits better to the experiemnt you are mading. 

Here we analice the performance of two well characterized and widely used alignenment-tools in RNA-seq data `STAR` and `hisat2` in contrast with the use of paired and unpaired data. 

For this we are going to use only the first SRR of the paired data of one alignment (in both tools) and then make the paired alignment also in both tools 


```{python}
# | echo: false

# chargue the libraries
import pandas as pd
import subprocess as sp
from xlrd.book import display_cell_address
from jupyter_core.migrate import regex
import os
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt


# first we load the metadata specific of our samples
metadata_df = pd.read_csv("data/Meatadata_male_bone_1_24M.tsv", sep="\t")
SRR_ids = list(metadata_df["srr_id"])
``` 

When parsing hisat2 output: 
Total aligned: The percentage of all the reads aligned  'Overall alignment rate'
Non aligned: the sum of the reads 'Aligned concordantly or discordantly 0 time' and 'Aligned 0 time'
Unique aligned: the sum of the reads 'Aligned concordantly 1 time' and 'Aligned 1 time' 
Multimaped: The sum of the reads that were'Aligned discordantly 1 time' and 'Aligned >1 times'
Time: the total time from the table met report  

For parsing the star output:
Total aligned: The division bewtwen the sum of 'Uniquely mapped reads number ' and 'Number of reads mapped to multiple loci ' and 'Number of input reads '
Non aligned: the sum of the reads  and 'Number of reads unmapped: other ', 'Number of reads unmapped: too short ' and 'Number of reads unmapped: too many mismatches '
Unique aligned: 'Uniquely mapped reads number '
Multimaped: The sum of the reads that were 'Number of reads mapped to multiple loci ' and 'Number of reads mapped to too many loci '
Time: The diference between 'Started mapping on ' and 'Finished on '

\newpage
>With that information we can run the alignments while we parse the aligner results: 

### Clean data 

```{python run_align}
# | eval: false

# Create the scsipts for the temporary paths and the scripts necessary
sp.run(["bash", "src/Generate_tempAlign.sh"])
# Iterate over all the SRR ids to run de corresponding alingments
for srr in SRR_ids:
    sp.run(["qsub", "src/jdl/Align_fatsq.jdl", "-S", srr, "-c"])
```

### Raw data

```{python}
#| eval: false

sp.run(
    [
        "bash",
        "src/Generate_tempAlign.sh",
        "-t",
        "temp_align_raw/",
        "-r",
        "results/alignments_raw/",
    ]
)

for srr in SRR_ids:
    sp.run(
        [
            "qsub",
            "src/jdl/Align_fatsq.jdl",
            "-S",
            srr,
            "-t",
            "temp_align_raw/",
            "-r",
            "results/alignments_raw/",
            "-e",
            "align_errors_raw/",
        ]
    )
``` 

### Merge the temporary results 
```{bash merge}
#| eval: false

#obtain the header 
path_temp=temp_align_raw/parsed_results/
header=$(head -1 "$path_temp"$(ls "$path_temp" | head -1))

#put the header in the final file
echo "$header" > results/alignments_raw/alignments_parsed.csv
#for evey temporary table we read it and we append it to the final file
for temp_parse in "$path_temp"*.csv; do
    #append without the heeader (number of register 1)
    awk 'NR!=1' $temp_parse >> results/alignments_raw/alignments_parsed.csv
done

```

With this we obtain the folloeing tables with the performance of the aligners: 

```{python read_tables}
# | echo: false
# | df-print: default

# fist we read the tables
Clean_df = pd.read_csv("results/alignments/alignments_parsed.csv")
Raw_df = pd.read_csv("results/alignments_raw/alignments_parsed.csv")

display(
    Clean_df.style.format(
        {
            "Total_aligned": "{:.3f}",
            "Non_aligned": "{:.3f}",
            "Unique_aligned": "{:.3f}",
            "Multimaped": "{:.3f}",
            "Time(min)": "{:.2f}",
            "Total_reads": "{:.0f}",
        }
    ).hide(axis="index")
)
```

\newpage

```{python}
# | echo: False

display(
    Raw_df.style.format(
        {
            "Total_aligned": "{:.3f}",
            "Non_aligned": "{:.3f}",
            "Unique_aligned": "{:.3f}",
            "Multimaped": "{:.3f}",
            "Time(min)": "{:.2f}",
            "Total_reads": "{:.0f}",
        }
    ).hide(axis="index")
)
```

\newpage

## Analysis 
With the performance data provided by the aligners we can see the diferrences not only between aligners also between paired and unpaired data. 

### Total aligned vs time 
The most common metric to evaluate the performance of an aligner is the total aligned reads, this is a key metric because it gives us an idea of how much information we are loosing in the alignment step, but also is important to evaluate the time that the aligner takes to do the alignment because this can be a bottleneck in the data processing.
```{python analysis}
# | echo: false

# ========================================================================
# Total aligned vs time
# ========================================================================

label_colors = {"hisat": "#161fcfff", "star": "#d11313ff"}

fig_scater, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 6))
fig_scater.set_facecolor("#d7ecfff1")
sns.scatterplot(
    data=Raw_df,
    x="Total_aligned",
    y="Time(min)",
    hue="aligner",
    palette=label_colors,
    style="Type",
    ax=axes[0],
)
axes[0].set_title("Total aligned vs time_Raw data")
axes[0].legend(loc="upper left", bbox_to_anchor=(1.02, 1))
axes[0].grid(True, linestyle="--", alpha=0.5)
axes[0].set_facecolor("#7a6ab949")

sns.scatterplot(
    data=Clean_df,
    x="Total_aligned",
    y="Time(min)",
    hue="aligner",
    palette=label_colors,
    style="Type",
    ax=axes[1],
)
axes[1].set_title("Total aligned vs time_Clean data")
axes[1].legend(loc="upper left", bbox_to_anchor=(1.02, 1))
axes[1].grid(True, linestyle="--", alpha=0.5)
axes[1].set_facecolor("#7a6ab949")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
``` 

We can see that hisat has a constant time of performance, this does not tell us so much about the cost in aligning paired or unpaired data, in the other hand we can note that the cost in time for star has a important difference between paired and unpaired data, something interesting is that even the more time used and consequently the more computational resources are used the less total aligned reads are, this can have so many reasons, one is that there are so many multimapped reads that does not represent a trouble when aligning unpaired reads but are better discriminated when using paired data, so other matrics like the unique aligned reads or multimaped reads can give us more insights about the noise the unpaired data is adding to the alignment. 

Other thing interesting in this case is that Star has a better performance using unpaired reads having more total aligned reads than hisat, even in less time, but whn using paired data the diferrence in total aligned reads does not seem to be big so the in terms of time hisat is better, but we have to see the other matrics to have a better idea of the performance of the aligners. 

Finally the use of clean or raw data appears to not have a big impact in the total aligned reads, but it is important to note that the time used for the alignment is higher when using raw data, this can be due to the fact that the aligner has to deal with more noise in the data and this can make the alignment process more complex and consequently more time consuming, also some samples like `SRR9126859` and `SRR9127010` were imposible to map using hisat2 in raw data so we continue the further analysis with the clean-data alignments.

\newpage

### Multimapping

For seeing why this difference between paired and unpaired reads number of relative mapped reads we can view the non aligned and multimaped reads, this can give us an idea of how much noise the unpaired data is adding to the alignment and how much of this noise is being discriminated when using paired data, this is important beacuse this noise could be false positive mapped reads to those genomics locations.

```{python multimaped}
# | echo: false

hisat_df = Clean_df[Clean_df["aligner"] == "hisat"]
star_df = Clean_df[Clean_df["aligner"] == "star"]

label_colors = {"UN": "#161fcfff", "PR": "#d11313ff"}

fig_scater, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(10, 6))
fig_scater.set_facecolor("#e0eef2f1")
sns.scatterplot(
    data=hisat_df,
    x="Multimaped",
    y="Non_aligned",
    hue="Type",
    palette=label_colors,
    ax=axes[0][0],
)
axes[0][0].set_title("Multimapped vs Non aligned reads hisat2")
axes[0][0].legend(loc="upper left", bbox_to_anchor=(1.02, 1))
axes[0][0].set_facecolor("#f0c5e3c2")

sns.scatterplot(
    data=hisat_df,
    x="Multimaped",
    y="Unique_aligned",
    hue="Type",
    palette=label_colors,
    ax=axes[1][0],
)
axes[1][0].set_title("Multimapped vs Unique Aligned reads hisat2")
axes[1][0].legend(loc="upper left", bbox_to_anchor=(1.02, 1))
axes[1][0].set_facecolor("#f0c5e3c2")

sns.scatterplot(
    data=star_df,
    x="Multimaped",
    y="Non_aligned",
    hue="Type",
    palette=label_colors,
    ax=axes[0][1],
)
axes[0][1].set_title("Multimapped vs Non aligned reads star")
axes[0][1].legend(loc="upper left", bbox_to_anchor=(1.02, 1))
axes[0][1].set_facecolor("#f0c5e3c2")

sns.scatterplot(
    data=star_df,
    x="Multimaped",
    y="Unique_aligned",
    hue="Type",
    palette=label_colors,
    ax=axes[1][1],
)
axes[1][1].set_title("Multimapped vs Unique Aligned reads star")
axes[1][1].legend(loc="upper left", bbox_to_anchor=(1.02, 1))
axes[1][1].set_facecolor("#f0c5e3c2")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

As the scatter plots show the use of paired reads as it is suposed to be seems to have a "robust" alignment having more toruble in solving those sequences that are multimapped, this reflects the higher number of non aligned reads when using paired reads, it can be see as a more cofident alignment but comparing this multimaped sequences with the unique aligned reads we can see that even the use of unpaired reads has a high rate of unique mapped reads it also has a high rate of multimaped reads, this can be a problem because these multimaped reads can be false positives and can add noise to the data, this is something that is being discriminated when using paired reads, so the use of paired reads seems to be more accurate in the alignment process. 

In this case we can see a clear trade off between the use of paired and unpaired reads, being the paired reads a more conservative approach but loosing some infomartion that the unpaired reads keep but with the risk of those multimapped reads that can be noise that the bivalent information of the paired reads can help to discriminate. 

Finally we can see that star has a better performance than hisat2, this because it has more unique aligned reads and less multimaped reads, so even to the more strict aligment it recovers more unique aligned reads with similar levels of multimapped ones. 

### Conclusion 
It is a fact that the time and resources invested in paired data aligment is higher than that for unpaired ones, so the question is clear, does it worth to invest this time and resources in the paired data aligment? Something clear is that this investment that in the most cases also involves more expensive experimental desings have a clear improve reducing multimapped reads that can be noise in the data, so the desision of using paired or unpaired data in the alignment step is a trade off between the time and resources invested and the accuracy of the alignment, so it is important to evaluate the specific needs of the experiment and the resources available before making a decision then the data also show us that the selection of the aligner coud be determinat in this king of trade offs and that is also experiment depedent desicion. 

In thiscase we can see that two of the most used aligners does not have a determinat deiference in selecting one or another, but it can be viewed that star has a better performance in the paired data that could be in order of the time and reosurcers expended in those alignments, in the other hand we can se that although star has clearly a better total of alignment-time performance in unpaired data, the multimapping evidence can tell us that using hisat could be a better desicion with a not a big diference also with it constant time performance. 

### Filtering the alignments
Once we have donde the aligments we can made the filtering of those with [samtools](https://www.htslib.org/doc/samtools.html) especially with the `samtools view` command, this command allows us to filter the alignments based on different criteria like the mapping quality, the flag of the alignment, etc. 

```{python filter_alignments}
# | eval: false

# iterate over all th e SRR ids to run de corresponding alingments
for srr in SRR_ids:
    sp.run(
        ["qsub", "src/jdl/clean_alignments.jdl", "-s", srr, "-p", "results/alignments"]
    )
``` 

```{bash verify_clean}
#| echo: false 

cat <(echo "There are $(ls results/alignments/clean/*HIun_clean.bam | wc -l) hisat clean un paired aligments $(ls results/alignments/clean/*HIpr_clean.bam | wc -l) hisat clean paired aligments") <(echo "There are $(ls results/alignments/clean/*SRun_clean.bam | wc -l) star clean un paired aligments $(ls results/alignments/clean/*SRpr_clean.bam | wc -l) star clean paired aligments") 

cat <(echo "There are $(ls results/alignments/clean/*HIun*.bai | wc -l) hisat clean un paired indexed aligments $(ls results/alignments/clean/*HIpr*.bai | wc -l) hisat clean paired indexed aligments") <(echo "There are $(ls results/alignments/clean/*SRun*.bai | wc -l) star clean un paired indexed aligments $(ls results/alignments/clean/*SRpr*.bai | wc -l) star clean paired indexed aligments")

```

Once we have made the analisys we can clean the temporary files 
```{bash clean_temp}
# | eval: false

#first we can remove the out files from the qsub process
rm -rf *.out
rm -rf *.err

#now we can remove the temporary folders
rm -rf temp_align/
rm -rf temp_align_raw/
rm -rf results/alignments/clean/errors/
```

# PseudoAligners 
Once the alignment the next step is the quantification of the reads this can be assed by tools like `featureCounts` from [Subread](https://subread.sourceforge.net/SubreadUsersGuide.pdf) this generates a table file with the counts for each gene in function of a reference genome annotation (GFF or GTF file) and the `bam` files generated for a common aligner, this generates a table of counts that is the milestone for the diferential expression analysis, but there is another approach in where the bam files are not generated and even the alignment euristic does not is follow in a traditional way. 

In the **pseudoAligmnets** the alogorithms, on big terms, use a de brujin graph to represent the reference genome as transcripts and the reads try to follow the paths on the possible transcripts based in a probability model, in wich the transcipt with the best probability is the one that is selected for the quantification of the reads, this has a upgrade in the time and resources used for the quantification and also reported a correlation near to 1 with the aligment approach, also it does not has to procces the bam files for quntification using `featureCounts` or similar tools, so it is a more direct approach for the quantification of the reads, they use tools like [tximport](https://bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/vignettes/tximport/inst/doc/tximport.html) for merging all the counts made by tools like [kallisto](https://kallisto.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) or [salmon](https://salmon.readthedocs.io/en/latest/salmon.html) in a single table for the following analysis. 


## Salmon 
Salmon is a pseudoaligner that could make both a `mapping-based` quantification and a `aligment-based` quantification, in this analysis we are going to use the `mapping-based` approach that is the one that use the **quasi-mapping** algorithm and is diferent to the traditional time consuming aligment approach.

>This indexes were provided so the salmon index procedure were not neccesary

## Kallisto 
In the case of Kallisto pseudo aligner we have to generate an index because it have not been provided by the class so we have to generate it using a multifasta file with al the transcriptome of the genome version we are working with, in this case the m36 version of the mouse genome, we can obtain this multifasta file from genecode and then generate the index with the `kallisto index` command.

```{bash kallisto_index}
# | eval: false 

#first we obtain the multifasta file with the transcriptome of the m36 version of the mouse genome
wget https://ftp.ebi.ac.uk/pub/databases/gencode/Gencode_mouse/release_M36/gencode.vM36.transcripts.fa.gz -O data/indexes/mouse_m36_transcriptome.fa.gz
#now we can download the gtf file with the annotation of the genes for the m36 version of the mouse genome
wget https://ftp.ebi.ac.uk/pub/databases/gencode/Gencode_mouse/release_M36/gencode.vM36.chr_patch_hapl_scaff.annotation.gtf.gz -O data/indexes/mouse_m36_annotation.gtf.gz 
#finally the comopresive genome fasta file 
wget https://ftp.ebi.ac.uk/pub/databases/gencode/Gencode_mouse/release_M36/GRCm39.genome.fa.gz -O data/indexes/mouse_m36_genome.fa.gz
#generate the directory for kallisto indexes
mkdir -p data/indexes/kallisto/
#generate the directory for kb indexes 
mkdir -p data/indexes/kb/

#now we generate the index with the kallisto index command 
conda run -n kallisto kallisto index -i data/indexes/kallisto/mouse_m36_transcriptome.idx data/indexes/mouse_m36_transcriptome.fa.gz -t 4 

#now we generate the index with the kallisto index command 
conda run -n bio_informatics kb ref -i data/indexes/kb/mouse_m36_transcriptome.idx -g data/indexes/kb/mouse_m36_t2g.txt -t 8 -f1 data/indexes/mouse_m36_transcriptome.fa.gz data/indexes/mouse_m36_genome.fa.gz data/indexes/mouse_m36_annotation.gtf.gz
```

The next step is to build the sample table for the kallisto quantification, this is a table with the sample names and the paths to the fastq files for each sample, this is necessary for the `kallisto quant` command that is the one that make the quantification of the reads, then we can run the `kallisto quant` program for all the samples. 

```{python build_sample_table}
# | echo: false

# data frames for the sample table
paired_kallisto_df = pd.DataFrame.from_dict(
    {
        "sample": metadata_df["name"].str.split("_").str[0]
        + "_"
        + metadata_df["months"].astype(str),
        "fastq_1": "data/"
        + metadata_df["srr_id"]
        + "/"
        + metadata_df["srr_id"]
        + "_1_clean.fastq",
        "fastq_2": "data/"
        + metadata_df["srr_id"]
        + "/"
        + metadata_df["srr_id"]
        + "_2_clean.fastq",
    }
)
unpaired_kallisto_df = pd.DataFrame.from_dict(
    {
        "sample": metadata_df["name"].str.split("_").str[0]
        + "_"
        + metadata_df["months"].astype(str),
        "fastq_1": "data/"
        + metadata_df["srr_id"]
        + "/"
        + metadata_df["srr_id"]
        + "_1_clean.fastq",
    }
)
unpaired_kallisto_df.sort_values("sample", inplace=True)
paired_kallisto_df.sort_values("sample", inplace=True)

# save the tables with the information for the kallisto quantification
paired_kallisto_df.to_csv(
    "data/indexes/kallisto/paired_kallisto_samples.csv",
    index=False,
    header=False,
    sep="\t",
)
unpaired_kallisto_df.to_csv(
    "data/indexes/kallisto/unpaired_kallisto_samples.csv",
    index=False,
    header=False,
    sep="\t",
)

display(paired_kallisto_df)
display(unpaired_kallisto_df)
``` 

For using the tables with the metadata we ares using the kb prgrams from the suite kallisto-bustools, this suite have a program called `kb ref` that allows us to generate the index for the quantification and also the table with the annotation of the genes for the following analysis, this is something that we have to do before running the quantification with kallisto.

```{bash}
#| eval: false

#output directory for the kb indexes
output_dir="results/pseudo_alignments/kb/paired"
mkdir -p $output_dir

#run kallisto for paired data 
echo "conda run -n bio_informatics kb count -i data/indexes/kb/mouse_m36_transcriptome.idx -g data/indexes/kb/mouse_m36_t2g.txt \
-x BULK --parity paired -o $output_dir --bootstraps 100 --tmp kb_paired -t 8 -m 12G data/indexes/kallisto/paired_kallisto_samples.csv"|\
qsub \
-N "kb_PR" \
-cwd \
-o "${output_dir}_kbPR.out" \
-e "${output_dir}_kbPR.err" \
-q default \
-pe smp 8 \
-l h_rt=04:00:00 \
-l h_vmem=16G 

#=====================================================================
#unpaired
#=====================================================================

output_dir="results/pseudo_alignments/kb/unpaired"
mkdir -p $output_dir

#run kallisto for unpaired data
echo "conda run -n bio_informatics kb count -i data/indexes/kb/mouse_m36_transcriptome.idx -g data/indexes/kb/mouse_m36_t2g.txt \
-x BULK --parity single -o $output_dir --bootstraps 100 --tmp kb_unpaired -t 8 -m 12G \
data/indexes/kallisto/unpaired_kallisto_samples.csv"|\
qsub \
-N "kb_UN" \
-cwd \
-o "${output_dir}_kbUN.out" \
-e "${output_dir}_kbUN.err" \
-q default \
-pe smp 8 \
-l h_rt=04:00:00 \
-l h_vmem=16G 
```

>The kb pipeline is better for single cell data so for comparison we are using the results from the old kallisto program

## Run

With the sample tables made for the kallisto quantification we can run the `kallisto quant` and `salmon quant` for all the samples, this will generate a table with the counts for each gene in function of the reference genome annotation and the bam files generated for a common aligner, this generates a table of counts that is the milestone for the diferential expression analysis. 

```{python salmon}
# | eval: false

# first we generate the temporary scripts for the salmon quantification
sp.run(["bash", "src/Generate_tempsPseudo.sh"])

# now we can make the aligment for each sample
for srr in SRR_ids:
    sp.run(["qsub", "src/jdl/pseudo_align.jdl", "-S", srr])
```

>Note here both pseudo aligners were run with 4 threads and 12 gigabytes of memory, also because both pseudo-aligners support boopstrapping for abundance estimation the number of boostrappings were set in 100, it is also important to note that in salmon the options --gcBias, --seqBias, --posBias, --validateMappings, --rangeFactorizationBins(in 4), were set.  

### Parse results 
With all the pseudo aligments made now we can asses the clean a parsed of both results and log files, this, essentially, is for deleting all the intermate bootstrapping files that kallist make and for parsing the stadistics from the pseudoaligment, those like oveall alignment, time and what pseudo-aligner done it. 
```{bash parse_pseudo}
#| eval: false

#Run the script for pasing the results
bash src/parsed_pseudo.sh -p results/pseudo_alignments
```


```{python read_tables}
# | echo: false
# | df-print: default

# fist we read the tables
res_df = pd.read_csv("results/pseudo_alignments/pseudo_align_stats.tsv", sep="\t")

display(
    res_df.style.format({"%_aligned": "{:.3f}", "Time(min)": "{:.3f}"}).hide(axis="index")
)
```

\newpage


## Analysis 
With the results that we have parsed we can make comparisons between both aligners and their times of run.



```{python plots}
# | echo: false

# ========================================================================
# Total aligned vs time
# ========================================================================

# add label to text in the dataframe
res_df["text_id"] = [id[len(id)-3::] for id in list(res_df["SRR_id"])]

label_colors = {"sal": "#161fcfff", "kal": "#d11313ff"}

fig_scater, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
fig_scater.set_facecolor("#d7ecfff1")
sns.scatterplot(
    data=res_df,
    x="%_aligned",
    y="Time(min)",
    hue="Aligner",
    palette=label_colors,
    style="Type",
    ax=ax,
)
for _, row in res_df.iterrows():
    ax.annotate(
        row["text_id"],
        (row["%_aligned"], row["Time(min)"]),
        textcoords="offset points",
        xytext=(5, 5),
        fontsize=8,
    )

ax.set_title("Total aligned vs time")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", bbox_to_anchor=(1.02, 1))
ax.grid(True, linestyle="--", alpha=0.5)
ax.set_facecolor("#7a6ab949")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

We can see in this plot that the general tendency of the results is, like as with the traditional aligners the time used for align or in this case "pseudo-align" paired data is more than the used for single end data, this diference is bigger for salmon. 

Another tendence that shares with traditional aligners is that the rate of aligment is more in the single end data. 

It is important to note that salmon has a more time consuming performance, this is because the time consuming od the procces for correcting the biases (--gcBias, --seqBias, --posBias) and for improving the abudance estimates (--validateMappings, --rangeFactorizationBins) we can see that it worth for improving the rate of aligment in th case of the options that correct the biases in the reads (for viewing the impact of abudance estimation options further analysis should be done). 

```{bash}
#| eval: false
#| echo: false

rm -rf *.err
rm -rf *.out 

rm -rf temp_pseudo_align/
``` 


# Big-wig files 

Another feature than can be analiced by the results of the aligments are the bigwig files, these can be generated by the `bamCoverage` program from the [deeptools](https://deeptools.readthedocs.io/en/develop/content/tools/bamCoverage.html) suite, this program allows us to generate bigwig files from the bam files generated by the aligners, this bigwig files can be visualized in genome browsers like [IGV](https://software.broadinstitute.org/software/igv/), [UCSC Genome Browser](https://genome.ucsc.edu/) or [IGB](https://www.bioviz.org) and can give us a visual representation of the coverage of the reads in the genome, this can be useful for seeing the quality of the alignment and also for seeing if there are any regions of the genome that are not being covered by the reads. 

This can be useful for seeing the overall diference between samples and between the coditions that we are trying to elucidate the expression diferences for that we are going to use only the paired end data that we have generated for both traditional aligners (`star` and `hisat`) this because are those that generate bam files. 

```{python bigwig}
#| eval: False

# run the bigwig processing for every SRR id
for srr in SRR_ids:
    sp.run(["qsub", "src/jdl/run_bamCov.jdl", "-p", "results/alignments/clean/", "-s", srr])
```

## Results 

For this brief analysis we the `IGB` genome browser we have selected arbitraly to samples of both conditions tested in our tissue (1 and 24 months) those are `SRR9126934` for 24 months and `SRR9127010` for 1 month with this samples we search in the genome browser the psoitions related with the gene **Ccl8** referent to the citokyne that is related with the inflametion that the authors in the original paper relate with the progression of life. 
```{python positions}
#| echo: false

t2g_df = pd.read_csv("data/indexes/kb/mouse_m36_t2g.txt", sep="\t", header=None)
t2g_df.columns = [
    "gene_id",
    "trans_id",
    "gene_name",
    "isoform",
    "chromosome",
    "start",
    "end",
    "strand",
]
# clean the ids
t2g_df["gene_id"] = t2g_df["gene_id"].str.replace(r"\..*", "", regex=True)
t2g_df["trans_id"] = t2g_df["trans_id"].str.replace(r"\..*", "", regex=True)
# find the one related with B2m
display(t2g_df[t2g_df["gene_name"] == "Ccl8"])
``` 

![Ccl8_genomeBW_hi_sr](../reports/Ccl8_genomeBW_HI_SR.png)

We can see that the expression of the gene Ccl8 is consistent with the data reported by the authors, it is important to note that thi is not a concluding result because here we are only viewing that the normalization a semi-quantification that bamCovarage do capture the difference acrros this 2 samples, and that diference is consitent in the data proccesed with `hisat2` and `star`, so this is a good insight for the next steps. 

# Quantification 
Once we have all the aligments and pseudoaligments done we can start assesing to construct the famous **count matrix** in wich the rows are the genes reported in our reference genome annotation (normally a `GTF` file) and the columns are the samples that we have in our experiment, this count matrix is totally made by `kb` (kallisto-bustools), for salmon is partially made because it uses uniques fastq files (paired or unpaired) but because it *maps* the reads to the transcriptome, those genes are already quantified so we only have to merge the tables generated for each sample in a single table with all the samples, this is something that is done by the `tximport` package in R. 

But for the case of the traditional aligners we have to use the `featureCounts` program from the [Subread](https://subread.sourceforge.net/SubreadUsersGuide.pdf) suite, this program allows us to generate a count matrix from the bam files generated by the aligners and the reference genome annotation (GTF file) and the bam files generated by the alignment of each sample to the reference genome, in the case of our analysis we have made 2 diferent aligments types (**paired** and **unpaired**) for each aligner (`hisat2` and `star`) having 4 diferent count matrixes to generate with featureCounts, this plus the ones that were made by by pseudoaligners. 

| Aligner | type | number_samples | results_path |
|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|
| hisat | paired | 8 | results/alignments/clean/*HIpr_clean.bam | 
| hisat | unpaired | 8 | results/alignments/clean/*HIun_clean.bam |
| star | paired | 8 | results/alignments/clean/*SRpr_clean.bam |
| star | unpaired | 8 | results/alignments/clean/*SRun_clean.bam |
| salmon | paired | 8 | results/pseudo_alignments/salmon/*salPR |
| salmon | unpaired | 8 | results/pseudo_alignments/salmon/*salUN |
| kallisto | paired | 8 | results/pseudo_alignments/kb/paired |
| kallisto | unpaired | 8 | results/pseudo_alignments/kb/unpaired | 

#### FeatureCounts 

```{bash featureCounts}
#| eval: false

# Run the feature counts for hisat2 and star for paired and unpaired data
bash src/run_featureC.sh -p results/alignments/clean/ -g data/indexes/mouse_m36_annotation.gtf.gz
```


#### Tximport 
```{r tximport}
# | eval: false

#import the libraries
library(tximport)
library(readr)

#load the required tables for the tximport
t2g_df <- read.table("data/indexes/kb/mouse_m36_t2g.txt", sep = "\t")
#keep only the columns t2g that matters for tximport (transcript id and gene id)
trans_2_gen_tx <- t2g_df[1:2]
#clean those columns
trans_2_gen_tx$V1 <- gsub("\\..*", "", trans_2_gen_tx$V1)
trans_2_gen_tx$V2 <- gsub("\\..*", "", trans_2_gen_tx$V2)

metadata_df <- read.table(
  "data/Meatadata_male_bone_1_24M.tsv",
  header = TRUE,
  sep = "\t"
)


for (aligner in c("salmon", "kallisto")) {
  #asign the file with the counts for each aligner
  if (aligner == "salmon") {
    count_file <- "quant.sf"
  } else {
    count_file <- "bs_abundance_99_clean.tsv"
  }
  #extract aligner abreviation
  align_sub <- substr(aligner, 1, 3)
  #create the vector with the paths for the results
  paired_files <- file.path(
    "results/pseudo_alignments",
    aligner,
    paste0(metadata_df$srr_id, paste0("_", align_sub, "PR")),
    count_file
  )
  single_files <- file.path(
    "results/pseudo_alignments",
    aligner,
    paste0(metadata_df$srr_id, paste0("_", align_sub, "UN")),
    count_file
  )
  #out the respectuve names to the vector
  sample_names <- paste0(metadata_df$name, "_", metadata_df$months)
  names(paired_files) <- sample_names
  names(single_files) <- sample_names
  #merge the salmon quantification results
  txi_paired <- tximport(paired_files, type = aligner, tx2gene = trans_2_gen_tx)
  txi_unpaired <- tximport(
    single_files,
    type = aligner,
    tx2gene = trans_2_gen_tx
  )
  #save the counts matrix
  write.csv(
    txi_paired,
    file.path(
      "results/count_mtr",
      paste0(align_sub, "_", "pr", "countMtr.txt")
    ),
    sep = "\t"
  )
  write.csv(
    txi_unpaired,
    file.path(
      "results/count_mtr",
      paste0(align_sub, "_", "un", "countMtr.txt")
    ),
    sep = "\t"
  )
}
```

\newpage

### Counts matrixes 
With all the previous steps we generated the count matrixes, this are 8 in total 2 for each aligner or pseudo aligner (hisat2,star,salmon,kallisto), those that visualized: 

#### Clean the feature counts matrixes 
The tables generated with feature counts have a comment line and the columns that have the counts are made with the path of the file that came from, so for that we are going to clean them  

```{bash}
#| eval: false

#clean for every SR or HI mtrx
for mtrx in results/count_mtr/[SRHI]*_*countMtr.txt; do
    #make the output name 
    out_file=${mtrx/.*/"_clean.txt"}
    #clean the matrix
    awk 'BEGIN{FS=OFS="\t"};
        NR == 1{next} 
        NR==2 {printf "%s%s", $1,"\t";
            for(i=7;i<=14;i++){
                sub(/.*\//, "", $i)
                sub(/_.*/,"",$i)
                printf "%s%s", $i,(i<14 ? "\t" : "\n")
            }
        } NR != 2 {print($1,$7,$8,$9,$10,$11,$12,$13,$14)}' "$mtrx" > $out_file
done
```


#### Hisat
```{python hisatPR}
#| echo: false
#| tbl-cap: "Count matrix generated by hisat2 with paired-end data"

count_mtrx = pd.read_csv("results/count_mtr/HI_pr_countMtr_clean.txt", sep="\t")

display(count_mtrx.head())
``` 


```{python hisatPR}
#| echo: false
#| tbl-cap: "Count matrix generated by hisat2 with single-end data"

count_mtrx = pd.read_csv("results/count_mtr/HI_un_countMtr_clean.txt", sep="\t")

display(count_mtrx.head())

``` 

#### star 
```{python hisatPR}
#| echo: false
#| tbl-cap: "Count matrix generated by star with paired-end data"

count_mtrx = pd.read_csv("results/count_mtr/HI_pr_countMtr_clean.txt", sep="\t")

display(count_mtrx.head())

``` 


```{python hisatPR}
#| echo: false
#| tbl-cap: "Count matrix generated by star with single-end data"

count_mtrx = pd.read_csv("results/count_mtr/HI_un_countMtr_clean.txt", sep="\t")

display(count_mtrx.head())
``` 

#### salmon
```{python hisatPR}
#| echo: false
#| tbl-cap: "Count matrix generated by salmon with paired-end data"

count_mtrx = pd.read_csv("results/count_mtr/sal_prcountMtr.txt")
counts_columns = [
    column
    for column in list(count_mtrx.columns)
    if "counts." in column or column == "Unnamed: 0"
]
mtrx_clean = count_mtrx[counts_columns].set_index("Unnamed: 0").rename_axis(None)
mtrx_clean.columns = [col.split(".")[1] for col in list(mtrx_clean.columns)]

display(mtrx_clean.head().style.format("{:.1f}"))
``` 


```{python hisatPR}
#| echo: false
#| tbl-cap: "Count matrix generated by salmon with single-end data"

count_mtrx = pd.read_csv("results/count_mtr/sal_uncountMtr.txt")
counts_columns = [
    column
    for column in list(count_mtrx.columns)
    if "counts." in column or column == "Unnamed: 0"
]
mtrx_clean = count_mtrx[counts_columns].set_index("Unnamed: 0").rename_axis(None)
mtrx_clean.columns = [col.split(".")[1] for col in list(mtrx_clean.columns)]

display(mtrx_clean.head().style.format("{:.1f}"))

``` 

#### kallisto
```{python hisatPR}
#| echo: false
#| tbl-cap: "Count matrix generated by kallisto with paired-end data"

count_mtrx = pd.read_csv("results/count_mtr/kal_prcountMtr.txt")
counts_columns = [
    column
    for column in list(count_mtrx.columns)
    if "counts." in column or column == "Unnamed: 0"
]
mtrx_clean = count_mtrx[counts_columns].set_index("Unnamed: 0").rename_axis(None)
mtrx_clean.columns = [col.split(".")[1] for col in list(mtrx_clean.columns)]

display(mtrx_clean.head().style.format("{:.1f}"))
``` 


```{python hisatPR}
#| echo: false
#| tbl-cap: "Count matrix generated by kallisto with single-end data"

count_mtrx = pd.read_csv("results/count_mtr/kal_uncountMtr.txt")
counts_columns = [
    column
    for column in list(count_mtrx.columns)
    if "counts." in column or column == "Unnamed: 0"
]
mtrx_clean = count_mtrx[counts_columns].set_index("Unnamed: 0").rename_axis(None)
mtrx_clean.columns = [col.split(".")[1] for col in list(mtrx_clean.columns)]

display(mtrx_clean.head().style.format("{:.1f}"))
```
